speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Paczynski, did you have any other encounters with the police, such as the one you just told us?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I had. And-- and apparently, this one was different. And it happened a few years before, when I was still living in Warsaw and attending the Warsaw high school. My aunt asked me to go the certain place in Warsaw and find out when the bus to Lublin, to the city of Lublin, is going, the schedule all of the buses. Apparently, this place where the buses were departing from Warsaw was in Jewish district, where-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] where-- where the Jews were living in Warsaw. Apparently, before the war, we heard no such meaning like ghetto or something like that. That was introduced by Germans when the Germans came. And we called the-- the-- the-- part of Warsaw where the Jews lived, we called it just-- just the Jewish district, but not a ghetto. So anyway, there was plaza there, and they were standing the buses, private buses, ran by Jewish people, two different location in Poland. And I went there. And I looked around. There was no office, and we're-- I didn't know whom to ask about the schedule of the buses to Lublin. But I notice a policeman standing there, ordinary Polish policeman. So I came to him. And I was dressed as a high school boy, because high school student had the uniform in Warsaw before the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I came-- so I came to him, and-- and nicely, I ask him if he could tell me when the bus, next bus to Lublin is going away. And he looked at me, and he said, why you not to ask when the bus to Palestine going away? I was stunned. I didn't know what to answer. And I went away. So apparently to me, later on, I realized that he was taking me as a Jewish boy. OK? And because he was somehow hostile toward Jews, so he had this nasty answer. Mr. Paczynski, were you involved in any resistance organization?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I was involved in the resistance organization when I left my family in-- when we lived before the war broke out. And I lived in-- in Warsaw city itself. And my-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] my landlord, I would say-- I was the renting or subletting one room in an-- in an apartment of-- occupied by the family. We knew those people from-- from-- from this place where my parents were living. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] he belonged already to the Polish organization, underground organization. And once he-- when I moved in, he asked me if I would like to join. So I said, yes, of course. At that time, for-- for people of my age, everybody was eager to-- to do something, which is-- was against Germans. OK. So I said, of course.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he arranged that there will be a meeting in-- in this apartment, his apartment. And that somebody will come and will take the-- how we call it-- allegiance to this organization and promise to be secret and so on, not reveal to anybody and so on.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there then you'll be also given the so-called-- the solemn name, or the-- I don't know-- certain secret name. And-- and indeed, it was a Sunday. And some-- somebody came. And there was also other people beside of this-- my landlord. And I spelled the allegiance formula. And I-- I joined this organization. The organization--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it called?

speaker: interviewee
text: The organization was [SIGHS] Z-W-Z, which is Z-W-Z, or Z-W-Z.

speaker: interviewer
text: What does that stand for?

speaker: interviewee
text: Which means-- it stands for Zwiazek Walki Zbrojnej, Association of-- of Armed Struggle. This organization later on was transformed. In 1943 in December, this organization was transformed to so-called Home Army, or Armia Krajowa, which was, or the ZWZ already was-- already-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] not organized, but controlled by the Polish government in exile in London.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you get involved with ZWZ?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was in 1940-- 1940. 1940, spring.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were you engaged in any other anti-Nazi activity?

speaker: interviewee
text: Later on, when I was still with this organization, accidentally in a streetcar, I met my leader of the Boy Scouts, which I knew him when I was in the high school in Warsaw. And after-- you know, a few words, he asked me if I were willing to help his organization, also underground organization, because I told him that I graduated high school, and I speak now in German because the language which I learned-- by six years I was learning-- and they-- [INAUDIBLE] yeah. Almost eight years German language at high school.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he asked me if I were willing to-- to give some lectures of the German language to his group of boys. And they were-- and I participated in this exercise. And there was a few meetings in very remote areas of city of Warsaw, where there were some young people coming. And I was just lecturing them on the German language. Did you take part in any other activities with the ZWZ?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. There-- there was-- during the 1940-- during the, I think, in '42 was ghetto uprising in Warsaw. Some of colleagues of the participants of this same organization, they ask me to help in transfer of arms to ghetto. To-- because at that time, I was very close to the area where-- where the ghetto was. Zolborska Street. And my participation in that was just to be a link in a-- in a chain of trafficking arms. Because I had such a-- a position that I-- I had in my office where I was working at desk, very close to the entrance. So from the street, you could just open the door, and there was my desk.

speaker: interviewee
text: So somebody was coming and leaving in the drawer of half-opened drawer in my desk, a package. I didn't look what it was in the package because I knew there is something, the ammunition or a revolver or something like that. It was-- there was the arms, the small arms smuggled to ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then after a few hours or-- or even sometimes the next day, there was coming the other person and was also result of asking any questions, and any talk, was grabbing this package and going away. But-- but I knew that this person was another link in-- in the smuggling of-- of arms. So that was my participation. I gave the-- the desk and the drawer and the access to it in order to transfer from one person to another person.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr Paczynski, did you take part in trying to save any Jews that were trying to escape?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] In a few-- in a few cases, in a few cases, I was introduced to somebody who needs-- who needed a-- a-- a document. Or you see, sometimes Jewish people, they could obtain identification document which was not completed. For example, there was no photograph there. And of course, if there was no photograph, there was no stamp, which, you know, one part-- on one part of that stamp goes on the photograph, the other one on the document itself.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I-- I did myself. Because I was quite a good draftsman, I did [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] falsified this red stamp. There was red stamp which goes on photograph and-- and document. A few-- few times I did that.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- and they were the people who were recommended to me. My friends and so on, they needed such a document. And I did a few things [INAUDIBLE] that. Then because when I was working on Zolborska Street, [SIGHS] there was-- there was a boy. Maybe he was 8, maybe 10 years old. And he was sometimes coming from the streets to-- to my office and trying to sell newspaper. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it was of-- several times he was coming to my office. And I-- well, of course, I-- I right away recognized that was a Jewish boy who-- who was doing that. This coming. And I-- I was impressed that he was-- in spite of his age, he was a very, very smart, eloquent, and so on.

speaker: interviewee
text: So there was-- there was a-- a cleaning lady. She was doing the fire in the oven during the winter when it was cold. And she was sweeping this office. So old, very simple lady.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- and this Jewish boy, after several times coming, and I was buying a newspaper or something, or helping him in very small things. And once he asked me that he has nowhere to sleep and if he could sleep when I am leaving this office. And I said, OK. There was an after room. There was another room, usually closed, without any windows. And there was a cot standing there. And I said, OK, you can sleep on this cot if you want. So I told him only to lock the door and sleep there. So he was doing that. He was sleeping there. And later on, somehow it was saw that I asked this sweeping lady that maybe she has a-- a corner in his-- in her [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] so-called apartment. She lived in some room in this-- in this house, that she could accommodate this-- this boy. And she agreed. Of course, the boy was telling her that he will pay for-- for this [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] place to sleep in-- in the corner of her room. And I know that she-- that he-- he was-- he was sleeping there. When I left Warsaw later on, and I came back, and I asked this lady what happened to this boy, and she said, that oh, he got a job at the airport in Warsaw. And I don't know what happened to him. He didn't show up anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you become involved with a Jew that you tried-- that you actually did help escape?

speaker: interviewee
text: Say again.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you become involved with a Jewish person who you actually did assist in escaping from Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, that was my present-- present-- my wife. She asked me to help her to escape from Warsaw. Because, you see, because of the situation and lack of-- of sympathy, a lack of, yeah, I would say the sympathy. Understanding what's going on and suffering of Jews, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] my present wife, she felt that she's not secure, not safe, staying, living in Warsaw.

speaker: interviewee
text: So she approached me to-- to help her to find a place or connections. You see in Warsaw, everything was based on connection, on if you knew somebody and so on. Sometimes you have to bribe this person or to give some money that some other person has to be bribed from it. So we-- that was every day. And I-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] acquaintance with my wife went to that precedence, or-- or that-- that she wanted to go away from Warsaw. Do you want me to elaborate that and use more details about that? First tell us her name. What was her at the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, at that time, her name was not real, but the assumed name, a false name, because she had already the documented identification, false document identification. And her name at that time was Sofia Palchek.

speaker: interviewer
text: But what was her real name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Real name was Lily Goldsobel. But at that time was hidden. And so [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I-- when I met her, and this meeting was just to get acquainted who-- who the person is-- this is through the friends. I had friends who knew my-- my wife even before the war, because they were going for vacations together-- well, together, to the same place. It was [NON-ENGLISH], not far from Warsaw, 30 kilometers. And the Goldsobel family was coming there for vacation, and-- and the family of-- of-- of her-- of this friend of mine was also coming there. And there was a girl. And she heard-- there was a girl and-- and-- and boy. So [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] who knew the family of Goldsobel. And apparently, this boy, he knew Lilian before the war. And-- and that's the-- the-- guy who-- [SIGHS] delivered this first document for Lilian to come out-- out of ghetto. And-- and that's what-- and at that time, it was 1943, spring 1943. He asked his sister to approach me and to find out if I could arrange something that she could go to Germany to-- to find some-- some job, let's say, in hotel or something, which will give her the chance to survive the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: So she did that. And I came. And that was my first [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] meeting her. And because I had to find right persons, you know, and therefore, it was required that I have to meet her several times.

speaker: interviewee
text: And doing this several times of meeting her, which the situation changed differently-- it would be different. I-- I got interested in her as a-- as a young girl. And I liked her. And-- and then later on, I offered to-- to stay with her and to go somewhere where we could have more chance to-- to survive together.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you wind up doing, planning to get her out of there?

speaker: interviewee
text: First, first we thought that maybe will be a good idea to go to the Russian partisans. We heard that in east of Poland, there are groups of Russian partisans which are fighting Germans. And we were quite-- both of us, she and I was quite naive and misinformed. And we didn't know how it looks like and so on.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we were-- well, we tried, and we-- we started to arrange somehow to get to that places, which means east of-- of the occupied Poland. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Apparently, it was-- well, almost ended in disaster, but somehow we managed to come out of it. And do you want to have this situation described in detail? Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. So because-- I will call Lillian-- Lillian is my wife and is the-- the Jewish girl which I met in 1943. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] In 1939, she went to the part of Poland which was occupied by Bolsheviks, by Russians. Because her uncle, a famous Jewish actor, Abram Morefski, he was at that time in Grodno. And Grodno was taken by the Russians. At that time, and it's 1939, there were-- the-- the mail was still going from both sides of-- of Poland. So the parents of Lillian, at the time being in ghetto, Warsaw ghetto, they got the message from Morefski that they should come to join him in Grodno, to-- to go through the border and go on the side of the Russian occupation.

speaker: interviewee
text: So anyway, the parents of Lillian decided that she will go there with her aunt, because the-- the parents of Lillian, they could not go because at that time, her-- her mother was still alive, and they don't-- didn't want to abandon her-- their mother. I think it was mother of-- of father. So they stayed ghetto, and also the-- the little child, sister of Lillian was there too. So only Lillian and this aunt went through illegal ways, through the-- we call it the green border, which means they hired somebody to-- to cross the River Bug on the other side, to go to the Russians, to the Russian occupation region. And they-- they-- finally, they reached the city Grodno.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this city, Grodno, she was living there. But meantime, not-- not long after, Morefski had to go to, I think, to Smolensk, to other city in Russia because he was performing in the territories. [INAUDIBLE]. And so-- and Lillian was at that time in school. So she was finishing the school.

speaker: interviewee
text: So she was exposed already to Soviet Union and-- and-- and the life in the Soviet Union. And as the young person, she didn't understand politics and everything. And not everything was known at that time. So she was quite a-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I-- I can't say-- well, not hostile to the Russians, which was a different from Polish people who were in-- in the occupied by Soviets part of Poland. They were always hostile to Russia, to the Soviet Union. But she was not.

speaker: interviewee
text: And, of course, she suggested to me that maybe I didn't know anything. I had absolutely no, at that time, knowledge or contact with Soviet Union and Bolshevism and communism or something like that. So I agreed to her-- with her, maybe that was a good idea to-- to look for the Russian partisans. So-- and through my connections, I found out that there is a group of people, Polish people in Warsaw, who were running a business in-- in this territories, the eastern part of Poland territories-- well, of course, German territories, occupied-- German occupied territories. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Apparently, there was a-- a company, and this-- this chief of this company or-- or president of the company was recruiting Polish crafters, like carpenters and-- and-- and smiths, locks, and so on, to go to that place-- apparently there was a city, Dubno in Polish, former Polish Yolhynia region-- and to work-- actually, it was to do maintenance for the German army. The-- every-- you know, almost every small-- even small city, there were some-- some German army units. And apparently, in this city, Dubno, there were larger German group of troops. And they were-- they were stationing there. And they needed some kind of maintenance. And they used the Polish workers to do the repairs in-- in their quarters and so on. So this company was looking for-- for somebody who could be a-- a manager of that group. And I wanted to go there.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Thank you.
